title: DAY 3 of the War | Can the Embassy Help Get Me Out?
text: So the embassy is straight over there somewhere. Oh man. Oh man. Oh man. Oh man. Hey. Hey. Hey. Hey. Hey. So I was hoping that I might see a pedestrian overpass. Google Maps is showing it as being a 36-minute walk, which is doable going down there. Oh boy. Oh boy. Hey guys. How's it going? So uh today is Monday, March 2nd, 2026. Currently 79° F out there. That is 26° C. This is day three of being stuck in Qatar here in the Middle East since the attack by the United States and Israel against Iran began on February 28th. And so as you can see especially for those who might not have seen previous videos, that I have a nice hotel room here at a great price, $40. Now people have mentioned that uh Qatar is offering to cover hotel costs for tourists who are trapped here. I just paid for another night just a little while ago. I'm not sure if I'm actually going to take advantage of that or not. I am just very grateful to be here. 40 bucks is a super good deal. It is a nice comfortable kind of perfect room, and so I don't know, maybe I will look into that later, but that isn't of the utmost importance for now. So, there is much to discuss as far as further updates, and I want to address some of the possible, you know, options for me to ultimately get out of here, but I don't want to spend too much time talking in the room here. I am about to get out and go to the embassy. So, I say embassies because as many of you know, I am a dual American Canadian, and so I am going to go to both the United States and the Canadian embassies, and maybe even the UK embassy because somebody just posted a comment saying that Canadians can make use of the UK embassy because Canada is part of the Commonwealth of Nations, and so it makes perfect sense for me to explore all possible options for ultimately, hopefully, getting out of here sometime relatively soon. So, I will discuss more stuff once I get out there, and maybe do some walking around. As you can see, then things are quite normal out there. People walking around, traffic. It is certainly quiet. On the subject of Iran attacking Qatar, just to put some numbers on it, then from what I found online on the first day, there were 65 Iranian missiles targeting Qatar, 64 of which were intercepted by the Qatari anti-missile defense system. There were also 12 drone attacks, 11 of which were thwarted by the anti missile system. And so, it is doing a great job, but not exactly perfect. Of the ones that apparently weren't shot down, then I'm not sure what the result was, but as best as I can figure out, there have been no serious actual casualties or any damage to buildings or anything here in Qatar. There is a video of flames and smoke and people running away from it. I'm not sure if that was perhaps the missile that wasn't intercepted or if that was debris from missiles that were shot down. It's a quite dramatic video here in Qatar, but nobody was hurt. But it is also of course always a concern. I have seen missiles out there during the day and during the night, heard the explosions, and they have shaken the hotel here. It is happening, it is real, the missiles are flying overhead, but so far I am quite safe. Okay, now time to get out of here and catch an Uber, which works here over to I think that I will start with the United States Embassy. I have no idea what to expect as far as what it's going to be like there. I could get there and it's closed, there's nobody around because they are advising people to shelter in place. Why am I going out if the US Embassy is advising people to shelter in place? Very reasonable question. I would say that that is overly cautious. As I just explained, there has been no damage or casualties here in Qatar. People are going about their normal daily business and getting to an embassy is one possible solution for me getting out of here. So this is of extreme importance, and so I'm going to go for it and see what it's like once I I there. Maybe it's closed, nobody around, maybe it's a huge crowd and I'm stuck waiting for hours. Only one way to really find out. I did call the Canadian Embassy yesterday. Their voicemail system said leave a message and we will call you back. They never called me back, not surprised about that. I'm sure that they are quite busy. So, really the best way to do this is just to get out there and go over and find out what the situation is and see what possible solutions there could be for me, whether that could be an ex creation flight, uh you know, an emergency flight going out because they can have special, you know, permission to depart even when the airport is closed. Or possibly getting out of here by land if they were to organize a bus or a shuttle that would cross into Saudi Arabia and then get to Riyadh or Dammam. There are lots of problems with that, which is one of the issues that I want to discuss later. Boats are not a possibility as far as I understand because they are also potential targets of Iran. There is a cruise ship that is docked here that was sailing through and is also stuck here waiting. So, anyways, let's get out of here. So, I'm waiting for the Uber. Something very strange just happened. I will explain once I get there. Here we are. So, my Uber driver was from Bangladesh. I didn't want to film at all in there and cause any kind of suspicion at all, but we had uh quite a conversation. His English was kind of mediocre, but uh anyways, we were talking a bit about uh what's going on. So, I am near the Embassy. However, I am not near the United States Embassy. So, something very bizarre happened when I tried to order the Uber. This morning I had checked on Uber and I had entered in Canadian Embassy and it had come up as possible to get a ride there. And then I went down to the lobby of the hotel and tried to order an Uber to the United States Embassy and it said no rides available. So I was confused thinking, well, maybe there just isn't [snorts] anything available at the moment. Although when I checked for the ride to the Canadian Embassy, there were a bunch of options that were all like one or two minutes away. So that seemed odd that all of a sudden there would be no rides available. So then I tried to get a ride to the Canadian Embassy and it came up. And that's the ride that I just got. And then just a minute ago, I checked again for rides on Uber to the United States Embassy, no rides available. So that is very interesting. Not sure exactly what to make of that, but uh I guess people don't feel comfortable going to the United States Embassy, something like that, and so they just aren't allowing it in the Uber app. So I don't know what I'm going to do after the Canadian Embassy. I could try to get a taxi over there because it's too far to walk, I believe. So not sure, but uh anyways, I am now a block away from the Canadian Embassy. See, I wanted to get a little bit away from there to do the filming. Anyways, now going to get to the Canadian Embassy, see if it's open, see what happens. So that was quick. The uh Canadian Embassy is not uh staffed at the moment today and he said maybe on Wednesday or Thursday. Instead, I am catching another Uber over to the British Embassy, which is another 10 minutes away. Just to be on the safe side, give that a shot, see if they might be there. It says on Google that they are open, but of course things are different now. So, that is the Qatar Art Center. I could not get in to the British Embassy. There was a security booth and you have to have a appointment. But, they did say that there is another office elsewhere that is actually closer to my hotel, and I got the name of that building, which I may check out later. But, after I uh figured out that I couldn't get into the British Embassy, I thought, well, let's still give the United States Embassy a try. And so, I did a little obvious trick, which is that I ordered an Uber to the Qatar Art Center, which is very close to the United States Embassy. But, there's one little problem. It looked quite close on the map, and it is quite close, but there is this massive divided freeway in the way. So, the Embassy is straight over there somewhere. Oh man. Oh man. Oh man. Oh man. Ayayayayay. So, I was hoping that I might see a pedestrian overpass. Google Maps is showing it as being a 36-minute walk, which is doable going down there. Uh boy, oh boy. I wonder if this could explain why it wasn't coming up on Uber because you can't actually access it maybe without some kind of a security uh clearance or whatever. So, I'm not sure what to do now other than maybe try ordering another Uber and make another point over on the other side there. And then I just got a notification from Air India. I had one more flight that I had booked for tomorrow evening. I am booking flights basically every day just in case the airport opens so that I have something reserved in advance, but just got notification that was canceled. No surprise there. I was expecting that to come in sometime today. So, uh as of now, I have no flights reserved. When I get back to my room, maybe I will try to reserve another one. Might as well because I am getting refunds on all these anyways. I guess I should stop walking if I'm going to order an Uber cuz it will be going that way. But, here's a good spot for an Uber to stop at least. No, no entry here. Just No, only here. Here is okay, yeah. Great. Thank you. Shukran. Something crazy is happening. It's raining. There were raindrops on the uh windshield. Anyways, I am looking at the US Embassy right now. Time to put this camera away. I'll tell you what happens. This is called the Arches Monument, a symbol of the cultural heritage of Qatar. At Bidda Park near the water looking out of the Persian Gulf or the Arabian Gulf. When you are here in these Gulf countries, it shows up on Google Maps as the Arabian Gulf. So, that was an interesting series of events. I picked another place to catch an Uber to right next to the US Embassy, and that worked. And so, I was walking over there, and then somebody comes running up behind me. This was a person who worked for the Embassy, who was just leaving. Now, they asked me not to mention them, so I'm not going to say if it was man or woman, or name, or anything like that. But, just want to say that uh I ended up talking with somebody from the Embassy who said that uh it is closed. They are advising shelter in place. So, at least I attempted and uh got some information in the process, including I got an email address from this person to send an email to in order to get in the system, get more information, be getting alerts going forward from here. So, all that was successful for that one thing of getting the email, which I probably could have just gotten online, but at least I now know the situation. I'm not missing out on anything by not going to the embassy. And so, I'm going to uh walk along here, nice uh fairly quiet spot, and head in the direction of my hotel. Still a long ways away. Maybe I'll catch another Uber. So, these Ubers are all really cheap. They're all like 10 to 15 Qatari riyal, and there's about three riyal to the US dollar. And so, they're all like $3 for these uh Uber rides of like 10 to 15 minutes or so. So, that is amazing because I just did like four or five of them, but it isn't going to add up to too much. So, I wanted to uh start here by offering some information and context as far as why I booked this flight to begin with. Now, if you've seen any of the other videos, then you know that I was on my way to India. I just had basically a layover here. Now, I had booked a flight into Doha and then was going to book the next flight as soon as I landed. I just hadn't made up my mind on that, but it was only going to be a day or so of a stop here. And so, as soon as I landed, then booked another flight out for that same evening. But, that was canceled as well as all my other attempts to book flights. But, as far as my ending up here to begin with, I made a another video already kind of addressing this, but I wanted to add something, which is that I am not the only one who was surprised when Iran decided to attack all of its neighbors, the Gulf states, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, and then UAE, Dubai, Abu Dhabi. So, I'm going to roll a clip, something that I just saw on CNN last night. It's unclear who is making strategic decisions, and they're making some mistakes, Dan. They're attacking the civilian areas of the Gulf and the Gulf states, the GCC states, they are UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, are uniting, and I would watch this over the coming days. Do they actually their air forces are now engaged in defense? Do they actually join some attacks into Iran in self-defense? I think that's a a key question. That was not, I think, on my bingo card when this started. I think the Gulf wanted to stay out of it. They're being drawn in, so that's something to watch over the coming Big mistake, I think, actually, Dan, because again, they don't seem that they that they haven't been attacking the Gulf states. The Gulf states wanted to stay out of it, as as Brett said. And so, there was an expert on the region who himself was surprised and confused as to why Iran has decided to fully wage war with all of its neighbors, Muslim nations that they are actually aligned with and friendly with to varying degrees, but for one thing, it is about the US military bases. But, there you go. Even an expert on the region did not expect that Iran was going to retaliate against the US and Israel by attacking all of its neighbors in the entire region. And so, this was partly the reason why I booked this flight was I thought this was going to be a war between the US and Israel and Iran. Here in Qatar, we're quite a ways south, I just did not realize that they would be engulfed in the war and especially that quickly. I mean, literally within whatever, an hour or two or something, then all of a sudden Iran is shooting missiles at all of these other Gulf countries along here. So, yes, I was aware of the build-up to a potential strike in Iran, but uh many people did not expect it to go this wide of a war this quickly. I mean, tourists in Dubai I'm sure most were not getting out because they weren't expecting to be involved in this either. So, it really is quite a stunning series of developments. Okay. Let's uh get to another spot maybe across the uh freeway there and keep on walking along the water. No pedestrian crosswalk across this massive uh road anywhere along here, so we'll uh keep on walking this way. So, now I want to address the uh options for getting out of here and some of the suggestions people have been giving in the comments. So, there are three ways out. By land, by air, by sea. Rule out by sea. So, speaking of which, there is a massive cruise ship there. I was just about to say that I had seen in the news that a cruise ship which was in the Gulf, and so even a cruise ship was not expecting this to happen in this part of the Gulf. They are stranded here, stuck waiting. Presumably because this is a safe zone here with the missile defense system versus being out in the open water. So, people have been mentioning that there are ferries like going to Bahrain, for example, and then from Bahrain then try to cross the land border into Saudi Arabia to Dammam. And the uh airports are apparently open in Dammam and in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. But, there are all kinds of problems with that one. First of all, almost certainly that ferry is not going because a boat out in the open water is a target. And then Bahrain is getting more hit than Qatar here. And then there is the issue of crossing that land border, any land border into Saudi Arabia because that is not a normal way that tourists enter Saudi Arabia. Flying in is fine. I've been to Saudi Arabia twice, to Jeddah and to Riyadh. And you don't even need a visa or I think it was visa on arrival, but anyways, you can just fly in and uh enter the country. Land border is a different situation. I've done a fair amount of research about this and it has been unclear if I would even be able to cross that border. Possibly I would need to get a visa. I looked online, it's $300. I would, of course, be more than happy to pay 300 bucks to get out of here. But, uh first of all, by sea, not practical. By air, obviously, is the preferable way. The airport is closed. That's all there is to that. There is no flying out of here until the uh airport opens other than repatriation flights, a diplomatic special plane. And so, that is the point of the embassies contacting them, getting on their list to find out if that could be an option in the future. So, by air, that is another option. Much preferred would just be that the airport opens in the coming days, and I can catch a flight out of here. But, then finally, the third option by land, and so lots of people are saying the war is going to last for weeks, 4 or 5 6 weeks. Just get out of there while you can. But, uh first of all, nobody knows how long this is going to last before things get back to something resembling normal. It's all over the place as far as uh information that people are spouting about what's going to happen. Well, it's just going to be a couple of days. It's going to be a month. I really think that literally nobody knows. Probably even Trump doesn't know at this point how long this is going to last as far as the US assault on Iran. So, I think there's no reason to take any kind of a timeline at this point seriously at all. It could be tomorrow, unlikely, but it could be in a few days. It could be 3 months before the airports open. But, uh there is every incentive for the airports to open sooner rather than later for the sake of these countries. These are powerful, influential countries, allies of the United States to varying degrees, but uh their economies, their tourism industry in particular, are being greatly affected by this. As soon as the airports do open up, people are going to be leaving in droves and they aren't going to be coming back anytime soon. And so this is already really devastated the region economically as it is, plus all the oil related stuff. Because this is affecting the shipping lanes, the Strait of Hormuz is closed by Iran because they control that. And so that is going to have an impact on the globe if that affects the price of oil. So there are all kinds of potential geopolitical ramifications that can occur and that will already occur even at this point. And so these countries have every incentive to try to put their pressure on the powers that are fighting and bring this to a conclusion to some extent as quickly as possible so that they can all get back to normal. So that effort will be ongoing of course on the part of all these countries over the coming days. So uh anyways, getting back to the land route option. Let's uh get away from this road up ahead a little bit. The flag of Qatar there. I don't know what this building is, but it looks very official and I don't want to be filming in front of it. So let's uh get beyond it. Looks like I might just be walking all the way to my hotel. So wrapping up the land route option and the various challenges and complications in attempting that because there are many. First, there is the border crossing potential obstacle. I don't know 100% that I can necessarily cross that border. With a visa online then sounds likely that I might be able to, but of course these are extraordinary circumstances. I don't know if they would be shutting down the border more or opening up the border more for foreigners. That would be hard to determine. It is an hour away to get to the border and then it is another 3 hours or so to Dammam or another 5 to 6 hours to Riyadh. So, you can do it in a day if you can do it, but uh can you cross that border? And how exactly are you going to cover that distance? Because this is not Europe or India where there is plentiful public transportation. Buses and trains and stuff like that. From what I found online then there is a bus that goes from Doha to Riyadh once per week. That's what it said online. A few different sources stated that. Maybe there's something else, but uh it's not like there's, you know, five buses a day covering that route. Is that bus even going to be going under the circumstances? And then there is the safety issue. So, Saudi Arabia is announcing that they may very well get involved in this conflict against Iran. Basically, the whole region seems to be concluding that Iran is a problem and needs to be dealt with. And so, they could get hit by Iranian missiles in Saudi Arabia. Their airports could get closed. So, I could go to all that trouble and then get there and then things shut down there. And then also, it is just the uh danger aspect of crossing just absolute stark desert for hours. I'm assuming there's security checkpoints. You would want to be 100% comfortable, confident with whoever was driving that they could be trusted. Probably the best option would be a taxi arranged by your hotel, so it's somebody that they know for certain is a trustworthy person. But, how many people are really going to be willing to do that crossing now when there's a war on? So, there are really just too many variables to seriously consider attempting that at this time. If this is going to drag on for weeks, then maybe I will start looking further into that. But, I don't think that that is really the way to go at this point to spend much more time looking into it than I already have to determine that. It is just very risky, uncertain, and I could get all the way there and then it's the same issue with airport closed when I'm here, things are relatively quite normal. I have a nice room at a cheap price, 40 bucks a night. And the airport could potentially open up any day. So, that makes a whole lot more sense for me to just stay here, be in touch with the embassy, and uh see what happens over the coming days. So, uh things are a fair amount uh busier here than the past couple days. I walked down here in previous videos. So, I'm very close to my hotel now. So, I think uh that is going to uh do it for today. But, uh man, it is just such pleasant weather here. So, that is one nice thing. This is unusual. I guess this is winter in the Middle East. Some clouds and a few drops of rain and slightly cool. But, uh better than super cold, better than scorching hot. I think the forecast is for it to get sunnier and a little warmer going forwards, but it's not going to be crazy hot. It is winter here. So, uh I think that wraps things up for now. In the uh coming videos, then I will attempt to do more of a tour of Qatar and show more of what there is to uh see and do here because there is a lot. As I have mentioned, this is my favorite city in this part of the world compared to Dubai, Riyadh, Kuwait, Bahrain, etc. Qatar is my favorite. So, of all the places to be stuck, this is one of the better places to be stuck. All right, that's all for now. Time to head back to my room, take a break, and get some stuff done.
